author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Ambrose Teaches that No One is Sinless in This World.
title: Ambrose Teaches that No One is Sinless in This World.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 54 [XLIX.]—Ambrose Teaches that No One is Sinless in This World.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Lastly, let him give good heed to his venerable bishop, when he is expounding the Prophet Isaiah, and says that “no man in this world can be without sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Now nobody can pretend to say that by the phrase “in this world” he simply meant, in the love of this world.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For he was speaking of the apostle, who said, “Our conversation is in heaven;” and while unfolding the sense of these words, the eminent bishop expressed himself thus: “Now the apostle says that many men, even while living in the present world, are perfect with themselves, who could not possibly be deemed perfect, if one looks at true perfection.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For he says himself: ‘We now see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know, even as also I am known.’

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Thus, there are those who are spotless in this world, there are those who will be spotless in the kingdom of God; although, of course, if you sift the thing minutely, no one could be spotless, because no one is without sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: That passage, then, of the holy Ambrose, which Pelagius applies in support of his own opinion, was either written in a qualified sense, probable, indeed, but not expressed with minute accuracy; or if the holy and lowly-minded author did think that Zacharias and Elisabeth lived according to the highest and absolutely perfect righteousness, which was incapable of increase or addition, he certainly corrected his opinion on a minuter examination of it.
